title: Pet-safe floor cleaner
subtitle: A white vinegar and water solution for hard floors in pet households
excerpt: White vinegar cuts through paw prints, grease, and light grime without the essential oils -- citrus, tea tree, and lavender are toxic to dogs and cats. Dilute into a spray bottle or a mop bucket. Safe on sealed stone, ceramic, laminate, and vinyl.
sourceNotes: Essential oil toxicity to dogs and cats: ASPCA Animal Poison Control Center guidelines. Acetic acid cleaning properties: standard household chemistry.

recipe.yieldDescription: 250 ml concentrate (dilute 1:3 with water to mop)
recipe.batchNotes: Scale up freely. A 1-litre bottle of concentrate covers several weeks of mopping.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make a large batch in a lidded bottle; it keeps indefinitely at room temperature.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Essentially indefinite at room temperature -- white vinegar does not spoil. Discard if the bottle develops a sediment or unusual smell.

glossaryTerms.definition: Distilled spirit vinegar at 5% acetic acid -- cheap and effective for cleaning. Do not use on natural stone (marble, limestone): the acid etches the surface.

content.text: Most natural floor-cleaner recipes include essential oils, but citrus, tea tree, lavender, and eucalyptus oils are all toxic to dogs and cats. This recipe uses only
and water, making it safe to use anywhere pets walk and then lick their paws. The acetic acid cuts through grease and surface grime, and the vinegar smell disappears once the floor dries.
Combine 125 ml of white vinegar with 125 ml of distilled water in the spray bottle. Swirl to combine. This is your concentrate.

As a floor spray: use the concentrate directly in the bottle, spray lightly on the floor, and mop or wipe immediately. As a mop-bucket solution: add 60 ml of concentrate to a bucket of hot water (roughly 2 litres) and mop as normal. The floor will smell of vinegar while wet; the smell dissipates as the floor dries. Rinse with clean water if the vinegar smell lingers.

Safe on sealed ceramic tiles, porcelain, vinyl, laminate, and sealed stone. Do not use on natural marble, limestone, or travertine -- the acid etches these surfaces. Do not use on unsealed or waxed hardwood floors, as vinegar can dull the finish over time. For waxed or oiled floors, a few drops of washing-up liquid in warm water is a safer option.
Keep at room temperature indefinitely. White vinegar does not spoil. Discard if the bottle develops a sediment or unusual smell, which would indicate contamination from a dirty bottle.
